editableNewDialogue.text: I keep hearing the terms “B-side,” “outtake,” and “demo” used interchangeably. Are they actually different?

They’re related but not the same.

- **B-side**: Historically, the extra track(s) on the flip side of a 7-inch single (or additional tracks on single releases later on). It’s a *release format* term.
- **Outtake**: A recording from an album’s sessions that wasn’t chosen for the final album tracklist (could be unfinished or fully finished). It’s a *selection/curation* term.
- **Demo**: A preliminary recording meant to sketch the song—often simpler, earlier lyrics, rougher sound. It’s a *stage-of-development* term.

A single track can be more than one of these (e.g., a demo released as a B-side).
So if I’m trying to collect “everything,” do I need every format of a single?

Not necessarily. Collectors usually separate two goals:

1) **All unique audio** (every distinct song/mix/live take)
2) **All variants** (every CD/7"/12"/cassette/promo with different artwork or sequencing)

If you only care about unique audio, you can compare tracklists and timings to avoid duplicates. A classic example: many 90s UK singles had “CD1/CD2” where the overlap is heavy, but one disc might have an exclusive live track or edit.

Common changes:
- Shorter intro/outro (fades earlier)
- Removed bridge/solo to hit ~3–4 minutes
- Cleaned-up lyrics (less common in rock, more common in pop/hip-hop)
- Less dynamic range difference is *not* typically called an edit; that’s more “radio mix” or a different master.

Sometimes labels also create an “edit” that’s literally a different structure (e.g., verse-chorus jumps).
I saw a listing for a “promo only” CD single. Does that usually have unique tracks?

Sometimes, but often promos just compile the main single version plus an instrumental, a clean version, or a shorter edit for stations. Unique content is more likely when the promo is an early pre-release (different mix) or when it includes a press-only interview track.

As a random historical note: some 90s promos were distributed with “no-commercial-value” markings and identical audio to the retail release—just different packaging.
When I’m sorting through those Summertime variants, I’m mostly after CD pressings—I’ve already got the three studio albums on CD and I’m trying to keep the singles in the same format.

Got it—sticking to CD makes it easier to standardize the collection. If you share the catalog numbers (or a Discogs link) for the specific CD variants you’re looking at, I can help you figure out which ones add unique B-sides versus duplicates.

Just to be clear, I’m not really “collecting complete discographies” as a hobby. With The Sundays I just wanted to make sure I wasn’t missing a couple stray B-sides—I'm not trying to track down every variant or do this for other bands.
Got it—so it’s not a general collecting preference. It was a one-off check for The Sundays so you could confirm you had the non-album tracks, not a broader “complete discography” thing.
Exactly. I’m not a completionist; I just didn’t want to overlook any unique songs.
